g.data-narration: Welcome to the Symmetric Cipher Model module. This foundational concept underlies all modern encryption.
g.text: Foundation of Classical and Modern Cryptography

g.data-narration: In digital security, the symmetric cipher uses one secret key for both encryption and decryption. It secures TLS, AES, and WPA2 networks.
g.text: In today's digital world, protecting information is paramount. The Symmetric Cipher Model is
the foundational framework where the SAME secret key encrypts and decrypts data. It's the
workhorse behind TLS, AES, and WPA2 - securing your web traffic and wireless networks.

g.data-narration: The five components are Plaintext, Encryption algorithm, Secret Key, Ciphertext, and Decryption algorithm. Each plays a vital role.
Plaintext is the original message. Encryption applies mathematical rules. The key must remain secret. Ciphertext is scrambled output.
The sender combines plaintext with the secret key using the encryption algorithm to produce ciphertext. Formula: C equals E of K and P.
The receiver uses the same key with the decryption algorithm to recover the original plaintext from ciphertext. Formula: P equals D of K and C.
The same key is used for both encryption and decryption. This symmetry gives the model its name. Both parties must share this key securely.
g.text: Notice: The SAME key K is used for both encryption and decryption! This "symmetry" is what
gives the model its name. Both sender and receiver must pre-share this secret key securely.

g.data-narration: Kerckhoffs's principle states security depends on key secrecy, not algorithm secrecy. The algorithm can be public, but the key must remain private.
g.text: "Security must depend only on the secrecy of the KEY, not the algorithm."
The algorithm can be public (published for review)
Only the key must remain private

g.data-narration: In Caesar cipher with key 3, A becomes D, T becomes W. Decryption shifts backwards by 3 using the same key to recover the original message.
g.text: Encryption: A+3=D, T+3=W, T+3=W, A+3=D, C+3=F, K+3=N
Decryption: Shift each letter BACKWARD by 3 positions using the SAME key K=3

g.data-narration: Secure symmetric encryption requires a strong algorithm that resists cryptanalysis, plus secure key distribution between communicating parties.
g.text: Must resist cryptanalysis. Brute-force should be
computationally infeasible even if algorithm is known.
Sender and receiver MUST share key securely BEFORE
communication. This is the KEY DISTRIBUTION PROBLEM.

g.data-narration: Modern symmetric ciphers evolved from DES in 1975, through 3DES, to AES in 2001, which remains the global encryption standard today.
g.text: From This Model Come Modern Standards

g.data-narration: In summary: five components with symmetric key usage, Kerckhoffs principle, and the key distribution challenge form the basis for DES, 3DES, and AES.
g.text: • Five Components: Plaintext, Encryption, Key, Ciphertext, Decryption
• Formulas: C = E(K,P) and P = D(K,C) - symmetric key usage
• Kerckhoffs: Security depends on KEY secrecy, not algorithm secrecy
• Challenges: Strong algorithm + Key distribution problem
• This model forms the basis for DES, 3DES, and AES
